The other one is more transactional, but both has a system and both have a process and both have very high average tickets. In just a few minutes we're going to be going over actually the six steps that you come up with in just a second. So one of the things that we have here is a bonus that comes with this program. It's something that we call Scripps. Scripps, it's a bonus that comes with the program. A lot of sales people, Dan, hate Scripps. They want to wing it. They think it hurts their creativity and all of that stuff. Let's address that because if we don't solve this problem, then the next six steps are going to be real difficult. So most dogs would prefer peeing and pooping anywhere they liked. That would be their natural instinctive preference. So Scripps is just, I feel a poop coming on. I take a poop. Nick often does that. Because when we domesticate this animal, we enforce a variety of rules about where they can poop and where they can pee. And so we determine that, we train that, and we enforce that. So sales people are much the same. Left to their own devices, they would rather just poop and pee whenever they feel like it, wherever they feel like it, etc. However, if we wish to domesticate them and we wish to have them be productive, we can't let them do that. So the fact that most sales people prefer, they argue for spontaneity, and they prefer not to script themselves, be scripted, be managed with Scripps, be taught Scripps. That tells us two things immediately. One, if most sales people feel that way, we know it's wrong. So even if we didn't know what was right, if we had never heard of Scripps, we would know that was wrong. Because whatever most of them are doing is wrong because most of them fail. Most of them barely make a living, they wash out, they tend to move down the sales ladder. So the guy who is selling fairly sophisticated products in the second year of his career is selling cars by the eighth year of his career. Why? Because he's a crappy salesperson. So we would know it was wrong even if we didn't know it was right. Then from a managerial standpoint, the dog analogy is valid. You wouldn't have to know what was right if you walked into someone's house and there was dog poop and piss everywhere, and the place smelled horrible. And you had to be careful where you walked to get to the couch if you had never had a dog. If you had never seen a dog before in your entire life, you just arrived from Mars. You would know this is not right. There is something wrong about this. Then if you went and looked at top performers, so in any sales field, sophisticated, unsophisticated, to his point, there's a process for selling a cheap suit to a teenager, and there's a process for selling. So sophisticated, unsophisticated, B2B consumer, there are many efforts. You pick a category of selling and you find the hundred in the country who are earning the highest incomes in that category, and you're going to find 98 of them are scripted. Now most of them will have scripted themselves, but they are scripted in stand-up comedy. There are a few, every generation, there's a few who literally go up there with no preconceived idea. They're improv people, they play with the audience, Robin Williams who just died, capable, Jonathan Winters, who he admired, capable. But we can name them in each generation because they are so few. And by the way, it's ever more dangerous to do that kind of comedy in today's political correctness environment. Everybody else has scripted pieces of shtick that they move in and out of. They have written them, every comedian is a writer, comedy writer. They write, Louis C.K. writes, Joan Rivers wrote, Seinfeld talks about that. Seinfeld is a writer, Larry David is a writer, these people write their stuff.
